title: Toad in the hole
subtitle: Pork sausages baked into risen Yorkshire pudding batter
excerpt: Pork sausages browned in a very hot tin of fat, then Yorkshire pudding batter poured over and baked until puffed and golden. The same rules as Yorkshire puddings apply: screaming hot tin, rested batter, do not open the door. Served with onion gravy.
sourceNotes: Toad in the hole is a British baked dish that appears in cookbooks from the 18th century, originally made with offal baked in batter. The sausage version became standard in the 19th century and is now the only common form. It is a midweek family supper rather than a Sunday roast component.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rest the batter for at least 30 minutes, up to overnight in the fridge.

content.text: Toad in the hole follows exactly the same logic as Yorkshire pudding: the tin must be smoking hot before the batter goes in, and the oven door must not be opened before the batter has set. The sausages are browned in the tin first, then the batter is poured around them while the tin is still extremely hot.
Whisk the {{eggs}} with the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} to a smooth paste. Gradually add the {{whole-milk}}, whisking until the consistency of thin cream. Rest for at least 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 220°C. Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} into a large, deep-sided roasting tin (approximately 30 x 20 cm). Place in the oven for 5 minutes. Add the {{sausages-pork}} to the hot tin and return to the oven for 15 minutes, turning once, until browned. Remove the tin from the oven. Working quickly, pour the rested batter around the sausages — it should sizzle dramatically on contact with the hot fat. Return immediately to the oven. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes without opening the door, until the batter is deeply golden, risen high at the edges, and set in the centre.

items.symptom: Batter flat and dense, does not rise
items.cause: Fat not hot enough, or batter poured too slowly
items.fix: The batter must hit screaming-hot fat and be poured in all at once. If the fat has cooled while you were browning the sausages, return the empty tin to the oven for 3 minutes before adding the batter
Do not open the oven for at least 20 minutes. The batter needs to set before any cold air enters

items.symptom: Sausages sitting in a pool of liquid fat in the batter
items.cause: Sausages not browned enough before batter was added
items.fix: Brown the sausages until they are deeply coloured, not just heated through. Pale, underbrowned sausages release fat into the batter as they cook

content.text: Toad in the hole is a midweek family supper, eaten with onion gravy and sometimes peas or a green vegetable. It is one of the most reliably popular dishes in British homes with children and one of the cheapest. The drama of the puffed batter arriving at the table contributes to its appeal.
